speaker: interviewer
text: August 13th, 1996. We are interviewing Alfred Gottschalk. The interviewer is Ann Mann Millin, in Cincinnati, Ohio, United States of America. And the interview is in English. My name is Ann Mann Millin. Today is August 13, 1996. We are in Cincinnati, Ohio. And I have the honor today of interviewing Dr. Alfred Gottschalk. Now are we running?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon. How are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Ready to go, how about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Would you state your name and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alfred Gottschalk, G-O-T-T-S-C-H-A-L-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a middle name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No middle name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: March 7, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city and if you know also the region and country were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oberwesel, O-B-E-R-W-E-S-E-L, am Rhein, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any nicknames as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know of.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what is your age today?

speaker: interviewer
text: 67, Thank you. You were born in Oberwesel in 1930. Could you give us a word picture of Oberwesel, as you remember it before 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Oberwesel was a picturesque sleepy little Rhineland village, noted mostly for its produce, which was rhine-- wine. And it was called [NON-ENGLISH]. So primarily the people in the city produced wines and liqueurs. Some also had other kinds of professions that dealt with leather goods. And they had fields and vineyards, the way people in the country live.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was nestled in a bend of the Rhine?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was nestled in a bend of the Rhine, between Bacharach and St. Goar.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the population, approximately, when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would be surprised if there were more than 3,500 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many of those Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: 37 families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Total of-- can you estimate the Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Total of maybe in '38-'39, most of them were elderly people. So I would say about 70-75, something like that. I was aware of only three other children in Oberwesel. By children, I mean youngsters below the age of 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish children, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the occupations of the Jews mostly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of them were [NON-ENGLISH], cattle dealers, some-- one was a baker. One was a butcher. They were on my street. That's how I remember them. And they were called [NON-ENGLISH], people who dealt in various kinds of either produce or products.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your street address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, 19-- [NON-ENGLISH], which was the street on which the [NON-ENGLISH] was located. That's at the head of the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you born in that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a hospital in Koblenz, but lived in that house until-- 19-- till my immigration in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how far away with Koblenz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Koblenz was about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 3/4 of an hour by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- oh, my goodness. So your mother had a 3/4 of an hour train ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Koblenz was sort of the big city you went to. And the next big city was Koln.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alright. Did you go to Koblenz or Koln often? Or was your life really centered in Oberwesel?

speaker: interviewee
text: You went to these places only for special occasions. I don't remember going to Koblenz ever, nor Koln. But I do remember visiting my grandparents in Niederzissen, and another set of grandparents in Gau-Odernheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were big trips. They were also a couple hours by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was your childhood like before 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I-- I don't remember very much of it, other than sort of a bucolic form of life. We lived in a family home, which had a beautiful garden that was rather tranquil. And-- um-- we had lots of relatives all the time coming to visit and stay. I remember our house, our home, as being lively, festive. I remember the Pesach celebration.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Pesach celebration like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was-- that was even more impressive than going to synagogue on Rosh Hashanah or Yom Kippur. Because my father and grandfather would get dressed in their-- what they call their sargenes. Sargenes is the Greek word for the robe you wear upon your ultimate retirement and death.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: White robe, and they would sit there with their slippers on, ready to go on the exodus again. And then during the day, they would dress up very festively in top hats, as they would go to synagogue. But the Seder was long, and was noisy, with family and relatives coming to visit. And I remember-- I remember my father and grandfather leading the service at the table, and my asking the four questions, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were an only child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was an only child, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the first grandchild or the oldest grandchild?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I was the oldest grandchild, right. There was one other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your cousin live there in Oberwesel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They lived in Gau-Odernheim. Because my family was split.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, tell us a bit about your family background. How long had the family lived in the region?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Gerson family who were the proprietors of the house in Oberwesel, and the family into which my father married, lived in Oberwesel for more than 400 years. I can assert that, because the gravestones in the friedhof, the Jewish cemetery, go back by family lines at least 400 years. Those are the dates we could make out. There were gravestones there that were so damaged that one could not make out the exact date. But 300-400 years is of a certainty. And they not only lived in Oberwesel itself, which I think was a rather later development, they lived in the back country around this once walled city, and owned land, vineyards and grazing land, around Oberwesel. So the family, the Gerson family and their relatives, they were like clans. You know, they had many, many relatives. Because in many instances in the smaller towns, cousins married, since there were very few available Jewish females. So they married cousins, and sometimes first cousins, which has happened in the Gottschalks and the Gerson family. So they kind of congregated, clustered around the smaller communities in which they lived, and would visit each other incessantly it seemed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a bit about your grandparents, both the Gottschalk and the-- your mother's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was fortunate. I had three sets of grandparents. My mother, who had her own set of parents, was adopted by her uncle and aunt after the Gersons, who are the uncle and aunt, lost their twin sons on the Russian front in World War I. One was named Alfred, the other Berthold. As it turns out, I'm named after one of them. And they approached my grandparents, the Trums in Gau-Odernheim, who were-- August Trum was the brother of Henrietta Wolff-Gerson.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she approached the grandparents, and asked whether they could have Erna come, my mother come, and live with them. My mother was one of five children. And they said, OK. And she lived with them. They liked each other very much. And then this became a permanent arrangement. They actually legally adopted her. And as I read through some of the papers in preparation for this interview, I saw how legalese this had become.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had then also my father's grandparents. They lived in Niederzissen. N-I-E-- Nieder, D-E-R-Z-I-S-S-E-N. His name was Jakob Gottschalk. Her name was Minna. They had Max, who was the oldest, Sauly, and Frieda; three children. Frieda married to Jakob Lillianthal.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Jakob Gottschalk and Minna Gottschalk of Niederzissen were another set of grandparents, and then the Gersons, and the Trums. So we negotiated these grandparents. And I remember as a child visiting them and having a wonderful time. My father's parents are very orthodox. My mother's parents were not so traditional. But they-- they lived a Jewish lifestyle in each instance. And in your family, you-- you kept Shabbat, and you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. The-- Oberwesel was a small town. And there was only traditional Judaism. There was no temple. There was a shul, to which all of the families went. Men sat downstairs, women upstairs. The services were conducted in-- almost entirely in Hebrew, far as I can recall. And you know, the holidays were celebrated in traditional ways. So it was a sort of traditional style, Rhineland style, as has been defined by Dr. Werner Weinberg, which showed that the Jews who lived in the smaller towns were much more Jewish in their orientation than those who lived in the larger cities, and could get lost in the bigger cities. So it was quite a traditional Jewish form of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that's not to say you weren't, in some degree, assimilated also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the language of the home was German. And the dress, of course, we dressed as our neighbors dressed. We also observed some of our neighbors' holidays, because we lived in close proximity, mostly to Catholic and Protestant families. And I can remember as a child the processions, the church processions going up and down [NON-ENGLISH], because they would emanate at the top of the street. They'd pass our house. And sometimes people would stop in, and wanted something to eat or use the facilities. So it was a very easy relationship for a long time. I can remember that, except on one-- one day a year when the whole town turned topsy turvy. And that was on [NON-ENGLISH]. This was the holiday which commemorated the canonization of Werner, who was [SIGHS] murdered in the 14th century by his nursemaid, as it turned out, but not before the Jews of Oberwesel had been accused of his death and his ritual murder. His blood was to have been used to bake matzahs. And if there's anything more reprehensible to imagine, I can't imagine it. Because Jews have a natural aversion to blood. But that was the libel. It was a blood libel. And Werner apparently swam upstream from Bacharach, which was the next town, to Oberwesel, the downstream town.

speaker: interviewer
text: He swam upstream in the Rhine.

speaker: interviewee
text: He swam upstream and pointed the finger to the synagogue in Oberwesel. And that's-- that was the legend as to how they knew that the Jews of Oberwesel were the murderers of Werner. And there was a pogrom, which has been widely documented. And the Catholic church made a Saint of Werner then. And each year, his-- his day would be commemorated. And that's the day my friends, whom I played with most of the year, would beat me up and accuse me of having killed Werner, and the association therefore also, again, of Jew as the Christ killer. So no matter what, you know, unreasonableness that was attached to this libel, it made no difference with the expression of violence. And the beatings sometimes were pretty bad. So after a while, Werner's Day I never left the house. Now what is unusual [SIGHS] in some respects is that when my wife and I went to Germany about 10 years ago, I took her on a visit through the town. And I hear by the wall, which was a passageway to one end of town, a mass going on. And it was a mass to-- it was Werner's Day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a mass to the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I went in to look at it. And the wall, which I'd never seen-- if had seen it was a child, I didn't remember it-- depicted the entire alleged incident. And when the Pope visited America in Miami, I happened to be sitting at the night of a festive dinner at the table of Cardinal Hildebrand's, who was in charge of Jewish-Christian relations. And I told him what I had seen and heard. He said, it's impossible. We desacralized Werner. All ritual murders where desacralized. I said, but Cardinal, I saw it and heard it. He said, it's impossible. I have a letter that he wrote me a month later, in which he said it's-- it was not only possible, it is still being practiced. He has commanded or ordered that it be stopped. But I will bet you dollars to donuts if we went to Oberwesel on the next Werner's Day, the mass would be recited. So I believe that this kind of residual anti-Semitism is built into the fabric of that society. And these legends don't die so quickly. And you can outlaw them. You can try to expunge them. But somehow they're embedded in the psyche of the people, which is a very frightening thing about these kinds of incidents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that even though your family was deeply involved in the life of the town, there was this ancient tradition there that upon which the Nazis could then build?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and there was, as we know, in the Rhineland itself over the course of the centuries, horrendous episodes of murder, pillage, and rape against Jews on a periodic basis into the 20th century-- with, you know, periods from the Weimar Republic on to the origins of Nazism over a short span of less than a decade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Your family was involved in the life of the town. I believe one of your relatives was, in fact, the leader of the town for a time. Would you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my grandmother, Henrietta Gerson, was elected mayor of Oberwesel in the late 1920s, which was rather remarkable; first of all, to have a woman as mayor, and secondly to have a Jewish woman as mayor. But she, by reputation, was one of the brightest people in town. And she had her term. I think also the fact that they had lost two sons who died for the fatherland, so to speak, that made a difference. But in the end, with the rise of Nazism, nothing that Jews had done for their country-- because they saw themselves as German citizens-- made any difference. They were singled out not because they were German Jews, but because they were Jews irrespective of the Germanness of-- of their life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the fact that my grandmother had been the mayor of Oberwesel, and that her two boys were killed fighting for Kaiser Wilhelm on the Eastern Front, in the end it made no difference. You were three years old when Adolf Hitler came to power, which means that your-- your grammar schooling was-- took place during the early Nazi period. What was it like to be a Jewish boy in the schools of Oberwesel at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't-- I remember only my expulsion. Because everything was kind of normal until then. And then one day in 1938, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a storm trooper, an SA or SS man-- I don't remember exactly-- I think a guy was garbed in a yellow uniform. So that would have made him SA-- came into the room and said, after reading a protocol, all Jewish children are hereby expelled [NON-ENGLISH], and then he yelled, hier raus!

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the Jewish children consisted of Ruth Lichtenstein and myself. Those were the only Jewish children in school at the time. And I remember with great humiliation, getting up and then the rest of the class chanting, [NON-ENGLISH]. So, from that point on, the word was out. You don't play with Jewish kids. However, within days after that event, I remember two nuns appeared at our doorstep in the evening one night, and invited my mother to send me to a Catholic school, which I did. I mean, I attended. And to their credit, they-- they took considerable risk to do that. So I used to come home at night, sitting down to dinner before saying the Motzi, the prayer over the bread, I crossed myself and then said the Motzi. So never really seeing any contradiction in this, until my mother, years later, pointed it out to me. And I still tell this to my Catholic friends with great joy. That you can-- you can have good people among the savagery that existed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I told you also that, actually, I wouldn't be talking to you altogether were it not for a righteous man who happened to have been a policeman, who warned my father by sending his son to tell him that there was a warrant out for his arrest that day, and that he should immediately leave the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was there a warrant for his arrest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the-- [SIGHS] the warrant was out for his arrest because [BACKROUND NOISE] he had an incident that occurred when we went into the back woods with horse and chaise, and-- the kid to whom my father was visiting his home, threw a bucket of water on the horse, because the horse was a Jewish horse. And the horse was, of course, shocked by the bucket of water. And my father took the kid by the earlobe, and marched him into the store of his father's house. And then his father gave him a good whack. But the fact was that my father had hit a child in a Hitlerjugend uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hitler Youth, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Hitler Youth uniform, which meant that he had committed a serious crime. And upon this kid reporting what had happened to him, a warrant went out for my father's arrest. It was posted. Now the town hall, as I understood it, had also the SS, the SA, the local police, and the state police all there together. So the local policeman happened to have seen the warrant posted, the arrest warrant, and he sent his son to warn my dad. And my father left overnight on a flatboat down to Holland. And he lived in Amsterdam for a period of about eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: If your father had not been warned, what would have this fate been that--

speaker: interviewee
text: They would have arrested him, and they probably would have beaten him to death for having put a hand onto a Hitlerjugend uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had there been other Jewish men in the town who had been arrested or maltreated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, oh sure, yes. Many [BACKROUND NOISE] of them went to Dachau, which is the concentration camp that most of them went to, the very first time around. So this is-- this is really a very serious matter-- that incident.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. This was, you said early in 1938 that your father escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so later in the year, of course, the November pogrom took place. What is your memory of that in Oberwesel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had forgotten it for many years. And then one day I was preparing a talk, which was on my inauguration as president of the college. And I had shown the talk to my son. And he said, you know, this is a nice talk. But there's nothing of you in here. Can't you-- don't you remember anything that motivated you? Then suddenly a memory came back to me of the Kristallnacht. And then I pieced it together. Because it started the night before, and there was beating on our doors, and windows, and no one actually entering the house. But it was a very frightening thing. It woke me up. Then the following morning-- we all then slept together in the living room. Following morning, my grandfather took me down to the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the place where the town gate had been originally and where the synagogue was located. And there was a brook in front of the synagogue at the time. And in the brook there was floating fragments of the prayer books and the Torah scrolls, hacked to pieces. And then we went inside the synagogue, which was completely tarred black. Now many of the synagogues were destroyed at Kristallnacht through burning. This synagogue, they couldn't burn because it was a triplex house, with non-Jews living on either side. So you couldn't burn just the synagogue, so they defaced the synagogue, tore it apart inside. And so my grandfather-- I remember in the-- wading into the Rhine River, and pulling out of it the scraps of the Torah that had been hacked to pieces, and the prayer books. And he gave them to me, and he said, here, take these. And I clutched them. I remember having this batch of wet parchment. And he said, one day we'll put the pieces back together again. And of course, that one day was never to be. Because thereafter, from the time my father left through Kristallnacht, the Nazis got very seriously involved with our family. Because they wanted to harass us, to the point that my father would be shamed into coming back. And had he done that, I wouldn't be talking now either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a real dilemma that the family faced. And my mother was interrogated every day. They came for her to humiliate her and to try to get to him. They kept asking, where is he? My mother would say, I don't really know. But my father would write to relatives in different cities, without writing for the first few months, to us in Oberwesel. Because he didn't want to endanger the family more than they were already endangered.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then people still traveled back and forth between Holland and Germany. So messages would come, telephone messages also. [SIGHS] So that my father then waited to go to Palestine, together with his cousin, Max Gottschalk. They sat in a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] special facility that had been prepared for Jews in transit, run by the Joint Distribution Committee, I think, awaiting for certificates which never arrived. At which point, they began to process immigration to America. We had relatives in Kansas City, also by the name of Gerson, who were related to the Gersons of Oberwesel. And they sent my father an affidavit, indicating that they would support him, and he would never become a charge to the United States. At that time, the United States had very severe immigration quotas. And it was not easy to enter the country as a refugee. So when he did make that voyage, he arrived in New York, and settled there. Then began the process of sending affidavits for us. And we received affidavits. But the Germans would not give me an exit visa, again, part of the harassment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop there, and pick this up on me next tape, all right?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
